llm_rationales.note: Selected professional, interdisciplinary, STEM-adjacent, and UI/UX because the page calls MIMS a 'two-year STEM-designated, full-time professional master’s program' for 'aspiring technology leaders, AI engineers, UX designers, and data-driven decision-makers' and describes an interdisciplinary curriculum bridging technology, people, and policy.
Did not add research because the evidence emphasizes professional preparation, internships, and final projects rather than a research-oriented degree path.

Did not add social_impact or entrepreneurship because the page mentions public good, policy, and innovation/entrepreneurship, but not strongly enough as the primary positioning theme for this program.
The program is described as a two-year full-time degree program and requires students to be registered for four consecutive academic semesters.
Two years at Berkeley's semester system corresponds to four standard semesters, so the appropriate category is four_semesters.
Costs are stated in US dollars on the MIMS funding page, so base_currency is USD.

Used the 'Total Tuition & Fees' annual figure of $33,880.50 for California residents as the tuition-and-mandatory-fees subtotal for cross-program comparison; this excludes housing, food, and other living expenses.
Used the same program-stated 'Total Tuition & Fees' figure of $33,880.50 as the defensible tuition-and-fees total in base currency for the degree scope supported by the evidence.
The overview page says there are five core courses but does not name them; named core courses were taken from the degree requirements page.

The evidence gives a first-year core and a semester-by-semester outline, but most electives are flexible and some required items are only described at the year level or without specific term placement.

The program explicitly says students customize their pathway and names multiple focus areas such as UX design, data science and machine learning, information policy, innovation and entrepreneurship, product management, and information privacy and security.

positioning.positioning_summary: MIMS is positioned as a two-year, full-time professional master’s program that prepares information professionals and technology leaders through an interdisciplinary curriculum spanning AI, UX, data systems, policy, ethics, and management.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: MIMS is a two-year, full-time, 48-semester-unit program with a structured first-year core, required advanced breadth in technology and social science/policy, and flexible electives used to build specialization. Students complete a group final project in the second year, and the program emphasizes interdisciplinary, project-based study across technical, social, policy, and design areas.

core_courses.course_summary: Introduces foundations of information organization and retrieval, including conceptual modeling, semantic representation, metadata design, classification, standardization, and information retrieval practices.
learning_outcomes: Understand conceptual modeling and semantic representation
Design vocabularies, metadata, and classification schemes
Apply standardization principles in information organization
Use information retrieval practices and applications
Analyze information in textual and non-textual formats

core_courses.course_summary: Introduction to information and information technology in society, including social impacts, workplace use, automation, and networked sociability.
learning_outcomes: Explain how technologies affect social groups and social interaction.
Analyze theories of technology in society.
Evaluate information technology in workplaces and work practices.
Compare quantitative and qualitative research approaches.
Discuss networked sociability, identity, and online communities.
Examine inequality, culture, ethics, and global technology contexts.

core_courses.course_summary: Required core course examining legal and ethical issues in information law and policy across commercial domains.
learning_outcomes: Analyze legal and ethical issues in information and communication technologies.
Evaluate privacy, freedom of expression, copyright, and consumer protection issues.
Assess how legal frameworks shape interfaces, data practices, and policies.
Reflect on the ethical impact of information technologies and professional responsibility.

core_courses.course_summary: Required first-year core course introducing Python programming and computation for computer science, data science, and information management.
learning_outcomes: Write interactive programs in Python
Analyze data with programming
Process text, graphics, and images
Simulate physical systems
Apply problem decomposition
Improve program efficiency and style

core_courses.course_title: Introduction to Data Structures and Analytics
core_courses.course_summary: Required first-year core course introducing data structures and data analysis in Python, with emphasis on coding best practices.
learning_outcomes: Understand fundamentals of data structures and data analysis in Python.
Apply best practices for writing code.
Use Python to analyze data.
Build on the first-half sequence begun in Info 206A.

core_courses.course_title: Directed Group Work on Final Project
core_courses.course_summary: Final project course integrating skills and concepts from the MIMS program through sustained, significant project work.
learning_outcomes: Integrate skills and concepts learned in the MIMS program
Formulate and carry out a sustained, coherent project
Produce a tangible work product
Practice project management
Present work in written and oral form
Work effectively in a multidisciplinary team when appropriate

curriculum.elective_requirements: Students must complete at least 48 semester units total, including at least one advanced technology course and one advanced social science or policy course. They must take a minimum of 40 units from the Info course catalog, which may include up to 6 units of individual study or group study. Up to 8 units from other UC Berkeley departments may count toward the degree.

elective_courses.course_id: Advanced social science or policy course
